6. When four dice are rolled randomly, the probability that the product of the four numbers obtained is a perfect square is $\qquad$ .

6. $\frac{25}{162}$.

Obviously, the number of 5s in four rolls must be even.
If all four rolls are 5, then their product is clearly a perfect square, and there is only 1 such case.

Below, we use a recursive method to consider the cases where there are exactly 0 or 2 fives in four rolls.

Let $a_{n}$ be the number of ways to select $n$ numbers from $\{1,2,3,4,6\}$ with repetition, such that the product of these numbers is a perfect square.
Notice that $a_{1}=2$.
For $n \in \mathbf{Z}_{+}$, consider the number of ways to select $n+1$ numbers such that their product is a perfect square. If the product of the first $n$ numbers is a perfect square, then the $(n+1)$-th number can only be 1 or 4, giving $2 a_{n}$ ways; if the product of the first $n$ numbers is not a perfect square, then the $(n+1)$-th number must be one of 2, 3, or 6, giving $5^{n}-a_{n}$ ways.
Thus, $a_{n+1}=2 a_{n}+\left(5^{n}-a_{n}\right)$
$=a_{n}+5^{n}\left(n \in \mathbf{Z}_{+}\right)$.
From this, we can recursively obtain
$$
a_{2}=7, a_{3}=32, a_{4}=157 \text {. }
$$

For the case where there are exactly 2 fives in four rolls, there are $\mathrm{C}_{4}^{2} \times 6$ ways to determine the positions of the fives.
Therefore, the required probability is
$$
\frac{1+6 \times 7+157}{6^{4}}=\frac{200}{1296}=\frac{25}{162} .
$$

The answer is $\boxed{\frac{25}{162}}$.
Third question: For all real numbers $x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{60} \in[-1,1]$, find the maximum value of $\sum_{i=1}^{60} x_{i}^{2}\left(x_{i+1}-x_{i-1}\right)$, where $x_{0}=x_{60}, x_{61}=x_{1}$.

Second question, Solution method one: First, take $a_{1}=a_{n}=\frac{1}{2}, a_{2}=a_{3}=\ldots=a_{n-1}=0$, then $\sum_{1 \leq i<j \leq n}(j-i) a_{i} a_{j}=\frac{n-1}{4}$.
Next, we prove by induction on $n$: $\sum_{1 \leq \mathrm{i}<\mathrm{j} \leq \mathrm{n}}(\mathrm{j}-\mathrm{i}) \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{j}} \leq \frac{\mathrm{n}-1}{4}$.
When $n=2$, $(2-1) a_{1} a_{2} \leq \frac{(a_{1}+a_{2})^{2}}{4}=\frac{1}{4}$, the conclusion holds.
Assume the conclusion holds for $n$, now consider the case for $n+1$.
By the induction hypothesis, we know:
$$
\sum_{1 \leq i<1 \leq n-1}(j-i) a_{i} a_{j}+\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}(n-i) a_{i}(a_{n}+a_{n+1}) \leq \frac{n-1}{4}
$$

And according to the condition, we have:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\sum_{1 \leq i<i \leq n+1}(j-i) a_{i} a_{j}-\left(\sum_{1 \leq i<i \leq n-1}(j-i) a_{i} a_{j}+\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}(n-i) a_{i}(a_{n}+a_{n+1})\right) \\
=[(n+1)-n] a_{n} a_{n+1}+\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}[(n+1-i)-(n-i)] a_{i} a_{n+1} \\
=a_{n+1}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{i}\right) \leq \frac{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} a_{i}\right)^{2}}{4}=\frac{1}{4}
\end{array}
$$

Thus, $\sum_{1 \leq i<j \leq n+1}(j-i) a_{i} a_{j} \leq \frac{n-1}{4}+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{n}{4}$.
In conclusion, the maximum value of $\sum_{1 \leq i<j \leq n}(j-i) a_{i} a_{j}$ is $\frac{n-1}{4}$.
Solution method two: First, take $a_{1}=a_{n}=\frac{1}{2}, a_{2}=a_{3}=\ldots=a_{n-1}=0$, then $\sum_{1 \leq i<j \leq n}(j-i) a_{i} a_{j}=\frac{n-1}{4}$.
On the other hand, let $S_{k}=\sum_{i=1}^{k} a_{i}$, then $S_{n}=1$, and
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\sum_{1 \leq i<j \leq n}(j-i) a_{i} a_{j}=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{i}^{2}+2 \sum_{1 \leq i<1 \leq n} j a_{i} a_{j}}{2}-\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} i a_{i}^{2}+2 \sum_{1 \leq i<j \leq n} a_{i} a_{i}}{2} \\
=\frac{n\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{i}\right)^{2}-\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{k} a_{i}\right)^{2}}{2}-\frac{\sum_{k=1}^{n}\left(\sum_{i=k}^{n} a_{i}\right)^{2}}{2} \\
=\frac{n S_{n}^{2}-\sum_{k=1}^{n-1} s_{k}^{2}}{2}-\frac{S_{n}^{2}+\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\left(S_{n}-S_{k}\right)^{2}}{2} \\
=\frac{(n-1) S_{n}^{2}-\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\left(s_{k}^{2}+\left(S_{n}-S_{k}\right)^{2}\right)}{2} \\
\leq \frac{(n-1) S_{n}^{2}-\sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \frac{\left[s_{k}+\left(s_{n}-s_{k}\right)\right]^{2}}{2}}{2}=\frac{n-1}{4}
\end{array}
$$

Third question,
Solution: Transform to get:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\sum_{i=1}^{60} x_{i}^{2}\left(x_{i+1}-x_{i-1}\right)=\sum_{i=1}^{60} x_{i}^{2} x_{i+1}-\sum_{i=1}^{60} x_{i}^{2} x_{i-1}=\sum_{j=1}^{60} x_{i} x_{i+1}\left(x_{i}-x_{i+1}\right) \\
=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{60}\left(\left(x_{i}^{3}-x_{i+1}^{3}\right)-\left(x_{i}-x_{i+1}\right)^{3}\right)}{3}=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{60}\left(x_{i+1}-x_{i}\right)^{3}}{3}
\end{array}
$$

Notice that, when $a \in[-2,2]$, $a^{3}-(3 a+2)=(a+1)^{2}(a-2) \leq 0$, hence $a^{3} \leq 3 a+2$. For any $1 \leq i \leq 60$, we have $x_{i+1}-x_{i} \in[-2,2]$. Therefore, we know:
$$
\sum_{i=1}^{60} x_{i}^{2}\left(x_{i+1}-x_{i-1}\right)=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{60}\left(x_{i+1}-x_{i}\right)^{3}}{3} \leq \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{60}\left[3\left(x_{i+1}-x_{i}\right)+2\right]}{3}=40
$$

When $\left\{\begin{array}{c}x_{3 k+1}=1 \\ x_{3 k+2}=0 \\ x_{3 k+3}=-1\end{array}(0 \leq k \leq 19)\right.$, the equality holds.

The answer is $\boxed{40}$.

6. [45] Let $A B C D$ be an isosceles trapezoid with $A B=1, B C=D A=5, C D=7$. Let $P$ be the intersection of diagonals $A C$ and $B D$, and let $Q$ be the foot of the altitude from $D$ to $B C$. Let $P Q$ intersect $A B$ at
R. Compute $\sin \angle R P D$.

Answer: $\frac{4}{5}$
Let $M$ be the foot of the altitude from $B$ to $C D$. Then $2 C M+A B=C D \Longrightarrow C M=3$. Then $D M=4$ and by the Pythagorean theorem, $B M=4$. Thus $B M D$ is a right isosceles triangle i.e. $\angle B D M=\angle P D C=\frac{\pi}{4}$. Similarly, $\angle P C D=\frac{\pi}{4}$. Thus $\angle D P C=\frac{\pi}{2}$, which means quadrilateral $P Q D C$ is cyclic. Now, $\sin \angle R P D=\sin \angle D C Q=\sin \angle M C B=\frac{4}{5}$.
An alternate solution is also possible:
Note that $A C \perp B D$ since $A B^{2}+C D^{2}=1^{2}+7^{2}=5^{2}+5^{2}=B C^{2}+D A^{2}$. Thus $P$ is the foot of the altitude from $D$ to $A C$. Since $D$ is on the circumcircle of $\triangle A B C$, line $P Q R$ is the Simson line of $D$. Thus $R$ is the foot from $D$ to $A B$. Then from quadrilateral $R A P D$ being cyclic we have $\angle R P D=\angle R A D$. So $\sin \angle R P D=\frac{4}{5}$.

The answer is $\boxed{\frac{4}{5}}$.

10. (6 points) As shown in the figure, two people, A and B, start from point $A$ at the same time and walk along the sides of the square $A B C D$ in the direction of the arrow. The side length of the square $A B C D$ is 100 meters. A's speed is 1.5 times B's speed. The two people meet for the first time at point $E$. Then, the area of triangle $A D E$ is larger than the area of triangle $B C E$ by square meters.

Example 2 In $\triangle A B C$, $\angle A B C=60^{\circ}, O, H$ are the circumcenter and orthocenter of $\triangle A B C$ respectively. Points $D, E$ lie on sides $B C, A B$ respectively, such that $B D=B H, B E=B O$. Given that $B O=1$. Find the area of $\triangle B D E$. ${ }^{[1]}$
(2008, International Youth Math City Invitational Competition)

Solve As shown in Figure 2, draw the diameter $A M$ of the circumcircle of $\triangle A B C$, and connect $C M, B M, C H$.

Since $H$ is the orthocenter of $\triangle A B C$, we have
$$
\begin{array}{l}
B H \perp A C, \\
C H \perp A B .
\end{array}
$$

Since $A M$ is the diameter, we get $M C \perp A C, M B \perp A B$.
Therefore, $B H \parallel M C, C H \parallel M B$.
Thus, quadrilateral $B H C M$ is a parallelogram
$$
\Rightarrow B H=M C \text {. }
$$

Since $O M=O C, \angle A M C=\angle A B C=60^{\circ}$, we know that $\triangle O M C$ is an equilateral triangle. Hence, $M C=O C$.
Therefore, $B D=B H=M C=O C=B O=B E$.
Thus, $\triangle B D E$ is also an equilateral triangle.
Given $B O=1$, we have $S_{\triangle B D E}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} B D^{2}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}$.

The answer is $\boxed{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}}$.

A point $P$ is inside a circle of radius $r$. The legs of a right angle with vertex $P$ intersect the circle at $A$ and $B$. Extend the triangle $B P A$ to form a rectangle $P A Q B$. What path does the point $Q$ follow as the right angle rotates around $P$?

Solution. Let the center of the circle be $O$, and $OP=d$. Let the feet of the perpendiculars from $O$ to the lines $AP$ and $BP$ be $F$ and $G$, respectively. By applying the Pythagorean theorem multiple times:

$$
OQ^2 = AF^2 + BG^2 = (r^2 - OF^2) + (r^2 - OG^2) = 2r^2 - OP^2
$$

Thus, the point $Q$ lies on the circumference of the circle centered at $O$ with radius $\sqrt{2r^2 - d^2}$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2024_05_02_f24fd5f034ae1d9bdfb3g-1.jpg?height=501&width=553&top_left_y=427&top_left_x=772)

Furthermore, it is also true that $Q$ traces the entire circumference of the circle as the right angle rotates around $P$. For this, let $Q'$ be any point on the circle, i.e., $OQ' = \sqrt{2r^2 - d^2}$. Let $A'PB'Q$ be the rectangle where $OA' = OB' =: r'$ (and $A'$ and $B'$ are the intersection points of the Thales circle of $PQ$ and the line perpendicular to $OK$ through its center $K$). Applying our previous result to the circle with radius $r'$ instead of $r$, we get that $OQ' = \sqrt{2r'^2 - d^2}$, so $r = r'$, which means $Q'$ originates from the right angle $A'PB'$.

The answer is $\boxed{Q}$.
18. Let $m$ and $n$ be relatively prime positive integers. If $m^{3} n^{5}$ has 209 positive divisors, then how many positive divisors does $m^{5} n^{3}$ have?

18. 217
Let $d(N)$ denote the number of positive divisors of an integer $N$. Suppose that the prime factorizations of $m$ and $n$ are $\prod\left(p_{i}^{a_{i}}\right)$ and $\prod\left(q_{i}^{b_{i}}\right)$ respectively. Observe that 209 has four positive divisors: $1,11,19,209$. If $m=1$, then $n^{5}$ would have 209 divisors. Thus, $d\left(n^{5}\right)=209$. However, it is known that $d\left(n^{5}\right)=\prod\left(5 b_{i}+1\right)$, but the latter implies that the remainder of $d\left(n^{5}\right)$ when divided by 5 is 1 , a contradiction.
Likewise, if $n=1$, then it implies that the remainder when $d\left(m^{3}\right)=209$ is divided by 3 is 1 , also a contradiction.
Thus, $m, n>1$, so $d(m), d(n)>1$. As $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime, then $d\left(m^{3} n^{5}\right)=$ $d\left(m^{3}\right) d\left(n^{5}\right)$. As the only way to factor 209 as a product of 2 integers greater than 1 is as $11 \cdot 19$, then $d\left(m^{3}\right)$ and $d\left(n^{5}\right)$ are 11 and 19 in some order. As the remainder when $d\left(m^{3}\right)$ and $d\left(n^{5}\right)$ is divided by 3 and 5 respectively is 1 , then $d\left(m^{3}\right)=19=(3 \cdot 6+1)$, and $d\left(n^{5}\right)=11=(5 \cdot 2+1)$. Thus, $m$ and $n$ can be expressed as $p^{6}$ and $q^{2}$ respectively. Therefore, $d\left(m^{5} n^{3}\right)=d\left(p^{30} q^{6}\right)=(30+1)(6+1)=217$.

The answer is $\boxed{217}$.

8. In $\triangle ABC$ with area 1, points $D$ and $E$ are on sides $AB$ and $AC$ respectively. Segments $BE$ and $CD$ intersect at point $P$. Points $D$ and $E$ move on $AB$ and $AC$ respectively, but satisfy the condition that the area of quadrilateral $BCED$ is twice the area of $\triangle PBC$. Find the maximum area of $\triangle PDE$.
(1992 Japan Mathematical Olympiad)

8. Let $\frac{A D}{D B}=\frac{s}{1-s}, \frac{A E}{E C}=\frac{t}{1-t}(0<s<1,0<t<1)$.

For $\triangle C D A$ and line $B P E$, applying Menelaus' theorem, we have $\frac{C P}{P D} \cdot \frac{D B}{B A} \cdot \frac{A E}{E C}=1$, which simplifies to $\frac{C P}{P D}=\frac{1-t}{t(1-s)}$.
For $\triangle B E A$ and line $C P B$, applying Menelaus' theorem, we have $\frac{B P}{P E} \cdot \frac{E C}{C A} \cdot \frac{A D}{D B}=1$, which simplifies to $\frac{B P}{P E}=\frac{1-s}{s(1-t)}$.
And $S_{B C E D}=S_{\triangle A B C}-S_{\triangle A D E}=\frac{1}{2} A B \cdot A C \cdot \sin A-\frac{1}{2} A D \cdot A E \cdot \sin A=(1-s t) \cdot \frac{1}{2} A B \cdot A C \cdot \sin A=(1-s t)$. $S_{\triangle A B C}=1-s t, S_{\triangle P B C}=\frac{1-t}{(1-t)+t(1-s)} \cdot S_{\triangle B D C}=\frac{1-t}{1-s t} \cdot(1-s) \cdot S_{\triangle A B C}=\frac{(1-s)(1-t)}{1-s t}$, given that $S_{B C E D}=2 S_{\triangle P B C}$, we get $1-s t=2 \cdot \frac{(1-s)(1-t)}{1-s t}$, thus $(1-s t)^{2}=2(1-s)(1-t)=2[1-(s+t)+s t]$. Also, $S_{\triangle P D E}=\frac{t \cdot(1-s) \cdot s \cdot(1-t)}{(1-s)(1-t)} \cdot S_{\triangle P B C}=s t \cdot S_{\triangle P B C}=s t \cdot \frac{1}{2} S_{B C E D}=\frac{1}{2} s t(1-s t)$.
This problem requires finding the maximum value of (2) under condition (1).
Using the AM-GM inequality $s+t \geqslant 2 \sqrt{s t}$ on (1), we have
$$
(1-s t)^{2}=2[1-(s+t)+s t] \leqslant 2(1-2 \sqrt{s t}+s t)=2(1-\sqrt{s t})^{2} \text {, }
$$
$\therefore \quad 1-s t \leqslant \sqrt{2}(1-\sqrt{s t})$, which leads to $(\sqrt{s t}-1) \cdot[\sqrt{s t}-(\sqrt{2}-1)] \geqslant 0$.
$\because 0<s, t<1$, then $\sqrt{s t}-1<0$, thus $0<\sqrt{s t} \leqslant \sqrt{2}-1$. Equality holds when $s=t=\sqrt{2}-1$.
$S_{\triangle P D E}=\frac{1}{2} s t(1-s t)=-\frac{1}{2}\left(s t-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{8}$.
When $s t=(\sqrt{2}-1)^{2}=3-2 \sqrt{2}$, $S_{\triangle P D E}$ reaches its maximum value, which is $-\frac{1}{2}\left(3-2 \sqrt{2}-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{8}=5 \sqrt{2}-7$.

The answer is $\boxed{5\sqrt{2}-7}$.

3、Function $y=\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{2+x}$ has the range of

Translating the above text into English, while preserving the original text's line breaks and format, the result is:

3、Function $y=\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{2+x}$ has the range of

Answer: $\left[0, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right]$.
Solution: Since $x \in[-1,1], y \geq 0$; and according to $y^{2}=\frac{1-x^{2}}{(x+2)^{2}}$, let $t=x+2$, then $y^{2}=\frac{1-(t-2)^{2}}{t^{2}}$, i.e., $\left(y^{2}+1\right) t^{2}-4 t+3=0$, considering it as a quadratic equation in $t$, its discriminant $\Delta=16-12\left(y^{2}+1\right) \geq 0$, we get $y^{2} \leq \frac{1}{3}$, $|y| \leq \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$, since $y \geq 0$, so $y \in\left[0, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right]$.

Alternative solution: $x \in[-1,1]$, let $x=\cos \alpha, \alpha \in[0, \pi]$, then $y=\frac{\sin \alpha}{2+\cos \alpha}, y \geq 0$, $2 y=\sin \alpha-y \cos \alpha=\sqrt{1+y^{2}} \cdot \sin (\alpha+\theta) \leq \sqrt{1+y^{2}}$, so $1+y^{2} \geq 4 y^{2}$, i.e., $y^{2} \leq \frac{1}{3}$, since $y \geq 0$, then $y \in\left[0, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right]$.

The answer is $\boxed{[0,\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}]}$.

$\mathrm{Az} A B=2 R$ diameter semicircle has a tangent drawn at point $B$. A secant from point $\mathrm{A}$ intersects the semicircle at $C$ and the tangent at point $D$, such that

$$
\overline{A C}^{2}+\overline{C D}^{2}+\overline{B D}^{2}=4 a^{2}
$$

Calculate the distance $A E=x$; $E$ is the projection of $C$ onto $A B$. For which value of $a^{2}$ does a solution exist and how many are there?

$R x$

$A B C$ is a right-angled triangle at $C$. According to the theorem for the legs of a right-angled triangle: $\overline{A C}^{2}=\overline{A B} \cdot \overline{A E}=$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2024_05_02_7b6d3d8cf10632a3928ag-1.jpg?height=255&width=423&top_left_y=263&top_left_x=840)

Since $C E \| D B$, we can write: $C D: E B=A C: A E$. Therefore,

$$
\overline{C D}^{2}=\frac{\overline{E B}^{2} \cdot \overline{A C}^{2}}{C E^{2}}=\frac{(2 R-x)^{2} 2 R x}{x^{2}}=\frac{2 R(2 R-x)^{2}}{x}
$$

Furthermore: $D B: C E=A B: A E$ so

$$
\overline{D B}^{2}=\frac{\overline{C E}^{2} \cdot \overline{A B}^{2}}{\overline{A E}^{2}}=\frac{x(2 R-x) 4 R^{2}}{x^{2}}=\frac{(2 R-x) 4 R^{2}}{x}
$$

Based on these, our equation is:

$$
2 R \cdot x+\frac{2 R(2 R-x)^{2}}{x}+\frac{(2 R-x) 4 R^{2}}{x}=4 a^{2}
$$

and after appropriate rearrangement:

$$
f(x) \equiv R x^{2}-\left(3 R^{2}+a^{2}\right) x+4 R^{2}=0
$$

The value of $x$ that corresponds to our problem must be real, positive, and less than $2 R$. The roots of the equation $f(x)=0$ are real if

$$
\left(3 R^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{2}-16 R^{4} \equiv\left(3 R^{2}+a^{2}+4 R^{2}\right)\left(3 R^{2}+a^{2}-4 R^{2}\right) \geq 0
$$

The first factor is always positive; the second cannot be negative. Therefore, it must be that $a^{2} \geq R^{2}$.

The product of the roots is $4 R^{2}$ and their sum is $\frac{3 R^{2}+a^{2}}{R}$, both positive, so both roots are positive. $\sqrt{4 R^{2}}=2 R$ is the geometric mean of the roots; this implies that one root is smaller and the other larger than $2 R$, or both equal to $2 R$ in the boundary case.

Accordingly, the problem has only one solution if $a^{2} \geq R^{2}$, which is

$$
x=\frac{3 R^{2}+a^{2}-\sqrt{\left(a^{2}+7 R^{2}\right)\left(a^{2}-R^{2}\right)}}{2 R}
$$

Sándor Horváth (Br. Kemény Zsigmond g. VI. o. Bp.)

The answer is $\boxed{\frac{3R^{2}+^{2}-\sqrt{(^{2}+7R^{2})(^{2}-R^{2})}}{2R}}$.

5. For different real numbers $m$, the equation $y^{2}-6 m y-4 x+$ $9 m^{2}+4 m=0$ represents different parabolas. A line intersects all these parabolas, and the length of the chord intercepted by each parabola is $\frac{8 \sqrt{5}}{9}$. Then the equation of this line is $\qquad$ .

5. $y=3 x-\frac{1}{3}$.

The parabola $(y-3 m)^{2}=4(x-m)$. The vertex $(m, 3 m)$ lies on the line $y=3 x$, so the required line must be parallel to it, and can be set as $y=3 x+b$. Take any parabola $y^{2}=4 x$ (when $m=0$), substitute $y=3 x+b$ into it to get $x^{2}+\frac{6 b-4}{9} x+\frac{b^{2}}{9}=0$. We can find that
$$
\left(x_{1}-x_{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{16-48 b}{81},\left(y_{1}-y_{3}\right)^{2}=\frac{16-48 b}{9} .
$$

From $\sqrt{\left(x_{1}-x_{2}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{1}-y_{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{8 \sqrt{5}}{9}$ we get $b=$ $-\frac{1}{3}$. Therefore, $y=3 x-\frac{1}{3}$.

The answer is $\boxed{y=3 x-\frac{1}{3}}$.

8. Given that the angle between skew lines $a$ and $b$ is $60^{\circ}$, through a point $P$ in space, draw a line $l$ that makes an angle $\alpha$ $\left(0^{\circ}<\alpha<90^{\circ}\right)$ with both $a$ and $b$, then the number of such lines $l$ is $f(\alpha)=$ $\qquad$

8. $f(\alpha)=\left\{\begin{array}{l}0\left(0^{\circ}<\alpha<30^{\circ}\right) \\ 1\left(\alpha=30^{\circ}\right) \\ 2\left(30^{\circ}<\alpha<60^{\circ}\right) \\ 3\left(\alpha=60^{\circ}\right) \\ 4\left(60^{\circ}<\alpha<90^{\circ}\right)\end{array}\right.$

Draw lines $a', b'$ through point $P$ parallel to lines $a, b$, respectively. Then lines $a', b'$ intersect at angles of $60^{\circ}$ and $120^{\circ}$. Therefore, when $0^{\circ}<\alpha<30^{\circ}$, line $l$ does not exist; when $\alpha=30^{\circ}$, line $l$ can only be the angle bisector of the angle between lines $a'$ and $b'$, so there is 1 line; when $30^{\circ}<\alpha<60^{\circ}$, through point $P$ in space, two lines $l$ can be drawn that form an angle $\alpha$ with both lines $a'$ and $b'$; when $\alpha=60^{\circ}$, line $l$ can be the angle bisector of the $120^{\circ}$ angle, plus the two lines from the previous case, making a total of 3 lines; when $60^{\circ}<\alpha<90^{\circ}$, through point $P$ in space, 4 lines $l$ can be drawn that form an angle $\alpha$ with both lines $a'$ and $b'$.
9. $\left[-\frac{1}{2}, 0\right) \cup\left(0, \frac{45}{8}\right)$

so $-\frac{1}{2} \leqslant x<0$ or $0<x<\frac{45}{8}$.

The answer is $\boxed{f(\alpha)={\begin{pmatrix}0(0<\alpha<30)\\1(\alpha=30)\\2(30<\alpha<60)\\3(\alpha=60)\\4(60}}$.

Example 2 As shown in Figure 2, in $\triangle A B C$, $A B=A C$, $\angle B A C=100^{\circ}$, point $D$ is on the extension of side $A B$, and $A D=B C$. Find the measure of $\angle B C D$. ${ }^{[2]}$

Solve as shown in Figure 2, construct an equilateral $\triangle A C E$, and connect $D E$.
Then $C E=C A=A E, \angle E A C=60^{\circ}$.
Thus, $\angle D A E=\angle B A C-\angle E A C$
$=40^{\circ}=\angle A B C$.
Also, $A E=A C=A B, A D=B C$, then
$\triangle D A E \cong \triangle C B A$.
Therefore, $D E=A C=C E$,
$\angle D E A=\angle C A B=100^{\circ}$.
And $\angle D E C=\angle D E A+\angle A E C=160^{\circ}$, so $\angle D C E=\angle E D C=10^{\circ}$.
Hence, $\angle B C D=\angle A C E-\angle A C B-\angle D C E=10^{\circ}$.
[Comment] Note that, for an isosceles triangle with a vertex angle of $100^{\circ}$, the base angles are $40^{\circ}$, and their difference is $60^{\circ}$. This is used to construct an equilateral triangle, making " $A D=B C$ " effective through congruent triangles.

The answer is $\boxed{10}$.

7. Given that for any real numbers $x, y$, the function $f(x)$ satisfies $f(x)+f(y)=f(x+y)+xy$.
If $f(1)=m$, then the number of positive integer pairs $(m, n)$ that satisfy $f(n)=2019$ is $\qquad$.

7.8 .

Let $y=1$, we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
f(x)+f(1)=f(x+1)+x \\
\Rightarrow f(x+1)-f(x)=m-x .
\end{array}
$$

Then $f(x)=f(1)+\sum_{k=1}^{x-1}(f(k+1)-f(k))$
$$
=m x-\frac{1}{2} x(x-1) \text {. }
$$

Also, $2019=f(n)=m n-\frac{1}{2} n(n-1)$, so
$$
n(2 m+1-n)=2 \times 3 \times 673 \text {. }
$$

When $n$ is even, $2 m+1-n$ takes 4 odd values. Similarly, when $n$ is odd, there are also 4.
In total, there are 8 pairs of positive integers that satisfy the condition.

The answer is $\boxed{8}$.

I3.1 In Figure 1 , a rectangle is sub-divided into 3 identical squares of side length 1 .
If $\alpha^{\circ}=\angle A B D+\angle A C D$, find the value of $\alpha$.

Method 1 (compound angle)
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\tan \angle A B D=\frac{1}{3}, \tan \angle A C D=\frac{1}{2} \\
0^{\circ}0 \\
\alpha=45 \\
\text { Method } 2 \text { (congruent triangles) }
\end{array}
$$

Method 2 (congruent triangles)
Draw 3 more identical squares $B C F E, C I G H, I D H G$ of length 1 as shown in the figure. $A L B D, D B E H$ are identical rectangles. Join $B G, A G$.
$B E=G H=A D$ (sides of a square)
$E G=H A=C D$ (sides of 2 squares)
$\angle B E G=90^{\circ}=\angle G H A=\angle A D C$ (angle of a square)
$\triangle B E G \cong \triangle G H A \cong \triangle A D C$ (SAS)
Let $\angle B G E=\theta=\angle G A H$ (corr. $\angle \mathrm{s} \cong \Delta$ 's)
$\angle A G H=90^{\circ}-\theta(\angle \mathrm{s}$ sum of $\triangle)$
$\angle A G B=180^{\circ}-\angle A G H-\angle B G E$ (adj. $\angle$ s on st. line)
$$
=180^{\circ}-\theta-\left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)=90^{\circ}
$$
$B G=A G$ (corr. sides $\cong \Delta ' s$ s)
$\angle A B G=\angle B A G$ (base $\angle$ s isos. $\Delta$ )
$$
=\frac{180^{\circ}-90^{\circ}}{2}=45^{\circ}(\angle \mathrm{s} \text { sum of } \triangle)
$$
$\alpha^{\circ}=\angle A B D+\angle A C D$
$=\angle A B D+\angle G B I$ (corr. $\angle \mathrm{s}, \cong \triangle$ 's)
$$
=45^{\circ} \Rightarrow \alpha=45
$$

Method 3 (similar triangles)
Join $A I$.
$A I=\sqrt{2}$ (Pythagoras' theorem)
$\frac{B I}{A I}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}}=\sqrt{2}, \frac{A I}{C I}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{1}=\sqrt{2}$
$\angle A I B=\angle C I A$ (common $\angle$ )
$\triangle A I B \sim \triangle C I A$ (2 sides proportional, included $\angle$ )
$\therefore \angle A C D=\angle B A I$ (corr. $\angle \mathrm{s}, \sim \Delta$ 's)
$\alpha^{\circ}=\angle A B D+\angle A C D=\angle A B I+\angle B A I$
$=\angle A I D($ ext. $\angle$ of $\triangle)$
$=45^{\circ}$ (diagonal of a square) $\Rightarrow \alpha=45$

Method 4 (vector dot product)
Define a rectangular system with $\overrightarrow{B C}=\mathbf{i}, \overrightarrow{B L}=\mathbf{j}$.
$\overrightarrow{A B}=-3 \mathbf{i}-\mathbf{j}, \overrightarrow{A C}=-2 \mathbf{i}-\mathbf{j}, \overrightarrow{A I}=-\mathbf{i}-\mathbf{j}$
$\overrightarrow{A B} \cdot \overrightarrow{A I}=|\overrightarrow{A B}||\overrightarrow{A I}| \cos \angle B A I$
$\cos \angle B A I=\frac{(-3)(-1)+(-1)(-1)}{\sqrt{(-3)^{2}+(-1)^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{(-1)^{2}+(-1)^{2}}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$
$\cos \angle A C D=\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \Rightarrow \angle B A I=\angle A C D$
$\alpha^{\circ}=\angle A B D+\angle A C D=\angle A B I+\angle B A I=\angle A I D($ ext. $\angle$ of $\Delta) \Rightarrow \alpha=45$
Method 5 (complex number)
Define the Argand diagram with $B$ as the origin, $B D$ as the real axis, $B L$ as the imaginary axis.
Let the complex numbers represented by $A B$ and $A C$ be $z_{1}$ and $z_{2}$ respectively.
$$
\begin{array}{l}
z_{1}=3+i, z_{2}=2+i \\
z_{1} \cdot z_{2}=(3+i)(2+i)=6-1+(2+3) i=5+5 i \\
\operatorname{Arg}\left(z_{1} z_{2}\right)=\operatorname{Arg}\left(z_{1}\right)+\operatorname{Arg}\left(z_{2}\right) \\
\alpha^{\circ}=\angle A B D+\angle A C D=\tan ^{-1} \frac{5}{5}=45^{\circ} \\
\alpha=45
\end{array}
$$

The answer is $\boxed{45}$.
Problem 3. Find all prime numbers $p$, for which the number $p\left(2^{p-1}-1\right)$ is a $k$-th power $(k>1)$ of a positive integer.

Solution. Let $p\left(2^{p-1}-1\right)=x^{k}(x>0$ is an integer $)$. It is clear that $p \neq 2$, i.e. $p=2 q+1$ is an odd number. Since $p / x$, then $x=p \cdot y\left(y\right.$ is a positive integer) and $\left(2^{q}-1\right)\left(2^{q}+1\right)=p^{k-1} y^{k}$. At least one of the numbers $2^{q}-1$ and $2^{q}+1$ is a $k$-th power of an integer, because they are relatively prime numbers.

1. Let $2^{q}-1=z^{k}$, i.e. $2^{q}=z^{k}+1$. If $k$ is even, then $z^{k}+1$ is not divisible by 4 . Hence $q=1, p=3$ and $p\left(2^{p-1}-1\right)=3^{2}$.

If $k=2 l+1$ then $2^{q}=(z+1)\left(z^{2 l}-z^{2 l-1}+\cdots-z+1\right)$, i.e. $z+1=2^{\alpha}$, where $0 \leq \alpha<q$. On the other hand

$$
2^{q}=\left(2^{\alpha}-1\right)^{2 l+1}+1=2^{2 \alpha} \cdot A+2^{\alpha}(2 l+1)
$$

( $A$ is an integer). The last equality contradicts with $\alpha<q$.

2. Let $2^{q}+1=z^{k}$, i.e. $2^{q}=z^{k}-1$. If $k$ is odd, we obtain a contradiction as in the previous case.

If $k=2 l$, then $\left(z^{l}-1\right)\left(z^{l}+1\right)=2^{q}$ and since $\operatorname{GCD}\left(z^{l}-1, z^{l}+1\right)=2$, we have $z^{l}-1=2$, i.e. $q=3, p=7, p\left(2^{p-1}-1\right)=7.63=21^{2}$.

The sought numbers are $p=3$ and $p=7$.

## Grade 11 .


The answer is $\boxed{p=3p=7}$.

4. In the set of real numbers, solve the equation:

$$
\sqrt[3]{x+2}+\sqrt[3]{3 x+1}=\sqrt[3]{x-3}
$$

4. By raising both sides of the equation to the third power (using the identity $\left.(a+b)^{3}=a^{3}+3 a b(a+b)+b^{3}\right)$, we get

$$
x+2+3 \sqrt[3]{x+2} \sqrt[3]{3 x+1}(\sqrt[3]{x+2}+\sqrt[3]{3 x+1})+3 x+1=x-3
$$

i.e.,

$$
3 \sqrt[3]{x+2} \sqrt[3]{3 x+1}(\sqrt[3]{x+2}+\sqrt[3]{3 x+1})=-3 x-6
$$

The expression in parentheses represents the left side of the original equation again, so it must be equal to $\sqrt[3]{x-3}$; by substituting this (and dividing both sides by 3) we arrive at

$$
\sqrt[3]{x+2} \sqrt[3]{3 x+1} \sqrt[3]{x-3}=-(x+2)
$$

Note, the last equation is not equivalent to the original (it is only a consequence of it); this means that for any solution found to the last equation, we must check whether it actually satisfies the original equation.

By raising both sides of the last equation to the third power, we get

$$
(x+2)(3 x+1)(x-3)=-(x+2)^{3}
$$

One solution is clearly $x=-2$. It is directly verified that this is indeed a solution to the original equation (both sides equal $\sqrt[3]{-5}$). Assuming $x \neq-2$, by canceling $x+2$ from both sides, we are left with $(3 x+1)(x-3)=-(x+2)^{2}$, i.e., $3 x^{2}-9 x+x-3=-\left(x^{2}+4 x+4\right)$, which is equivalent to $4 x^{2}-4 x+1=0$. Solving this quadratic equation, we get $x=\frac{4 \pm \sqrt{(-4)^{2}-4 \cdot 4}}{8}=\frac{4 \pm 0}{8}=\frac{1}{2}$. Let's check if this solution satisfies the original equation. On the left side, we get $\sqrt[3]{\frac{1}{2}+2}+\sqrt[3]{3 \cdot \frac{1}{2}+1}=2 \sqrt[3]{\frac{5}{2}}$, and on the right side, $\sqrt[3]{\frac{1}{2}-3}=\sqrt[3]{-\frac{5}{2}}$, so $x=\frac{1}{2}$ is not a solution to the original equation.

Therefore, the only solution is $x=-2$.

The answer is $\boxed{-2}$.

5. (10 points) Adding the digits of a number to get a new number is called one operation. A number that can become 6 after several such operations is called a "good number". The number of "good numbers" not exceeding 2012 is $\qquad$, and the greatest common divisor of these "good numbers" is $\qquad$.

5. (10 points) Adding the digits of a number to get a new number is called one operation. A number that can become 6 after several such operations is called a "good number". The number of "good numbers" not exceeding 2012 is $\qquad$ 223, and the greatest common divisor of these "good numbers" is $\qquad$.

【Analysis】The "good numbers" in the problem actually refer to the numbers less than or equal to 2012 that leave a remainder of 6 when divided by 9, i.e., the sequence 6, 15, 24, etc. The greatest common divisor of 6 and 15 is the greatest common divisor of this sequence.

【Solution】Solution: $(2014-6) \div 9+1$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=1998 \div 9+1 \\
=222+1 \\
=223 \text { (numbers); }
\end{array}
$$
The greatest common divisor of 6 and 15 is 3, so the greatest common divisor of all "good numbers" is 3.
Answer: The number of "good numbers" not exceeding 2012 is 223, and the greatest common divisor of these "good numbers" is 3.
Therefore, the answers are: 223, 3.

The answer is $\boxed{223,3}$.

Given a natural number $k$, find the smallest natural number $C$ such that
$$\frac C{n+k+1}\binom{2n}{n+k}$$is an integer for every integer $n\ge k$.

1. **Claim and Initial Check:**
   We claim that the smallest natural number \( C \) such that 
   \[
   \frac{C}{n+k+1} \binom{2n}{n+k}
   \]
   is an integer for every integer \( n \ge k \) is \( C = 2k+1 \).

2. **Lower Bound for \( C \):**
   To establish a lower bound for \( C \), consider the case when \( n = k \):
   \[
   \frac{C}{k+k+1} \binom{2k}{k+k} = \frac{C}{2k+1} \binom{2k}{2k} = \frac{C}{2k+1}.
   \]
   Since \( \binom{2k}{2k} = 1 \), the expression simplifies to \( \frac{C}{2k+1} \). For this to be an integer, \( C \) must be divisible by \( 2k+1 \). Therefore, \( C \ge 2k+1 \).

3. **Verification for \( C = 2k+1 \):**
   We need to verify that \( C = 2k+1 \) works for all \( n \ge k \). Consider the binomial coefficient:
   \[
   \binom{2n}{n+k}.
   \]
   We need to show that 
   \[
   \frac{2k+1}{n+k+1} \binom{2n}{n+k}
   \]
   is an integer for all \( n \ge k \).

4. **Expression Simplification:**
   Consider the difference of binomial coefficients:
   \[
   \binom{2n}{n+k} - \binom{2n}{n+k+1}.
   \]
   Using the definition of binomial coefficients, we have:
   \[
   \binom{2n}{n+k} = \frac{(2n)!}{(n+k)!(n-k)!}
   \]
   and
   \[
   \binom{2n}{n+k+1} = \frac{(2n)!}{(n+k+1)!(n-k-1)!}.
   \]
   Therefore,
   \[
   \binom{2n}{n+k} - \binom{2n}{n+k+1} = \frac{(2n)!}{(n+k)!(n-k)!} - \frac{(2n)!}{(n+k+1)!(n-k-1)!}.
   \]

5. **Common Factor Extraction:**
   Factor out the common term \(\frac{(2n)!}{(n+k)!(n-k)!}\):
   \[
   \binom{2n}{n+k} - \binom{2n}{n+k+1} = \frac{(2n)!}{(n+k)!(n-k)!} \left(1 - \frac{(n+k)!(n-k)!}{(n+k+1)!(n-k-1)!}\right).
   \]
   Simplify the fraction inside the parentheses:
   \[
   1 - \frac{(n+k)!(n-k)!}{(n+k+1)!(n-k-1)!} = 1 - \frac{(n-k)}{(n+k+1)} = \frac{2k+1}{n+k+1}.
   \]
   Thus,
   \[
   \binom{2n}{n+k} - \binom{2n}{n+k+1} = \binom{2n}{n+k} \frac{2k+1}{n+k+1}.
   \]

6. **Conclusion:**
   This shows that 
   \[
   \frac{2k+1}{n+k+1} \binom{2n}{n+k}
   \]
   is indeed an integer for all \( n \ge k \). Therefore, the smallest \( C \) that satisfies the condition is \( C = 2k+1 \).

The final answer is \( \boxed{2k+1} \).

Consider the permutation of $1,2,...,n$, which we denote as $\{a_1,a_2,...,a_n\}$. Let $f(n)$ be the number of these permutations satisfying the following conditions:
(1)$a_1=1$
(2)$|a_i-a_{i-1}|\le2, i=1,2,...,n-1$
what is the residue when we divide $f(2015)$ by $4$ ?

1. **Define the problem and recurrence relation:**
   We are given a permutation of $\{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$, denoted as $\{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n\}$, and we need to find the number of such permutations, $f(n)$, that satisfy:
   - $a_1 = 1$
   - $|a_i - a_{i-1}| \leq 2$ for $i = 2, 3, \ldots, n$

   The recurrence relation for $f(n)$ is given by:
   \[
   f(n) = f(n-1) + f(n-3) + 1
   \]

2. **Analyze the recurrence relation modulo 4:**
   We need to find the residue of $f(2015)$ modulo 4. To do this, we will compute the first few values of $f(n) \mod 4$ and identify any repeating pattern.

3. **Compute initial values:**
   Let's compute the initial values of $f(n) \mod 4$:
   \[
   \begin{aligned}
   f(1) & = 1 \\
   f(2) & = 1 \\
   f(3) & = 2 \\
   f(4) & = f(3) + f(1) + 1 = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 \equiv 0 \mod 4 \\
   f(5) & = f(4) + f(2) + 1 = 0 + 1 + 1 = 2 \mod 4 \\
   f(6) & = f(5) + f(3) + 1 = 2 + 2 + 1 = 5 \equiv 1 \mod 4 \\
   f(7) & = f(6) + f(4) + 1 = 1 + 0 + 1 = 2 \mod 4 \\
   f(8) & = f(7) + f(5) + 1 = 2 + 2 + 1 = 5 \equiv 1 \mod 4 \\
   f(9) & = f(8) + f(6) + 1 = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 \mod 4 \\
   f(10) & = f(9) + f(7) + 1 = 3 + 2 + 1 = 6 \equiv 2 \mod 4 \\
   f(11) & = f(10) + f(8) + 1 = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 \equiv 0 \mod 4 \\
   f(12) & = f(11) + f(9) + 1 = 0 + 3 + 1 = 4 \equiv 0 \mod 4 \\
   f(13) & = f(12) + f(10) + 1 = 0 + 2 + 1 = 3 \mod 4 \\
   f(14) & = f(13) + f(11) + 1 = 3 + 0 + 1 = 4 \equiv 0 \mod 4 \\
   \end{aligned}
   \]

4. **Identify the repeating pattern:**
   From the computed values, we observe the pattern:
   \[
   1, 1, 2, 0, 2, 1, 2, 1, 3, 2, 0, 0, 3, 0
   \]
   This pattern repeats every 14 terms.

5. **Determine the position of $f(2015)$ in the pattern:**
   Since the period is 14, we need to find the position of $2015$ modulo 14:
   \[
   2015 \mod 14 = 13
   \]
   Therefore, $f(2015) \equiv f(13) \mod 4$.

6. **Find the value of $f(13) \mod 4$:**
   From our pattern, $f(13) \equiv 3 \mod 4$.

The final answer is $\boxed{3}$.

Problem 10.6. In a right triangle $ABC$ with a right angle at $A$, the altitude $AH$ is drawn. A circle passing through points $A$ and $H$ intersects the legs $AB$ and $AC$ at points $X$ and $Y$ respectively. Find the length of segment $AC$, given that $AX=5$, $AY=6$, and $AB=9$.

Answer: 13.5.

Solution. Let $K$ be the second intersection point of the given circle with side $BC$ (Fig. 9). Since $\angle AHK=90^{\circ}$, segment $AK$ is the diameter of this circle, and $\angle AXK = \angle AYK = 90^{\circ}$. Therefore, $AXKY$ is a rectangle, and $XK = AY = 6$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2024_05_06_2fdcf97aa9799d0d4cd6g-38.jpg?height=422&width=497&top_left_y=92&top_left_x=476)

Fig. 9: to the solution of problem 10.6

From the condition, it follows that $XB = AB - AX = 9 - 5 = 4$. Note that right triangles $XBK$ and $ABC$ with a common acute angle $B$ are similar. Therefore,

$$
\frac{4}{6} = \frac{XB}{XK} = \frac{AB}{AC} = \frac{9}{AC}
$$

from which we find $AC = 13.5$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2024_05_06_2fdcf97aa9799d0d4cd6g-38.jpg?height=423&width=493&top_left_y=929&top_left_x=478)

Fig. 10: to the solution of problem 10.6

Another solution. From the inscribed quadrilateral $AXHY$, it follows that $\angle XHY = 180^{\circ} - \angle XAY = 90^{\circ}$. Note that $\angle CHY = 180^{\circ} - \angle XHY - \angle BHX = 90^{\circ} - \angle BHX = \angle AHX$ (Fig. 10). This means that in similar right triangles $AHC$ and $BHA$, points $Y$ and $X$ correspond to each other, that is, they divide the corresponding sides $AC$ and $AB$ in the same ratio (this can also be derived from the similarity of triangles $CHY$ and $AHX$). Therefore,

$$
\frac{6}{CY} = \frac{AY}{CY} = \frac{BX}{AX} = \frac{4}{5}
$$

from which we find $CY = 6 \cdot \frac{5}{4} = 7.5$ and $AC = AY + CY = 6 + 7.5 = 13.5$.

The answer is $\boxed{13.5}$.

There are three flies of negligible size that start at the same position on a circular track with circumference 1000 meters. They fly clockwise at speeds of 2, 6, and $k$ meters per second, respectively, where $k$ is some positive integer with $7\le k \le 2013$. Suppose that at some point in time, all three flies meet at a location different from their starting point. How many possible values of $k$ are there?

[i]Ray Li[/i]

1. We need to find the values of \( k \) such that the three flies meet at a location different from their starting point. The flies travel at speeds of 2, 6, and \( k \) meters per second, respectively, on a circular track with a circumference of 1000 meters.

2. Let \( t \) be the time in seconds when the flies meet again at a location different from their starting point. The positions of the flies at time \( t \) can be described by the following congruences:
   \[
   2t \equiv 6t \equiv kt \pmod{1000}
   \]

3. For the flies to meet at the same point, the positions must be congruent modulo 1000. This gives us the system of congruences:
   \[
   2t \equiv 6t \pmod{1000}
   \]
   \[
   6t \equiv kt \pmod{1000}
   \]

4. From the first congruence, we have:
   \[
   2t \equiv 6t \pmod{1000} \implies 4t \equiv 0 \pmod{1000} \implies t \equiv 0 \pmod{250}
   \]
   This implies that \( t \) must be a multiple of 250.

5. From the second congruence, we have:
   \[
   6t \equiv kt \pmod{1000} \implies t(k - 6) \equiv 0 \pmod{1000}
   \]
   Since \( t \equiv 0 \pmod{250} \), we can write \( t = 250m \) for some integer \( m \). Substituting this into the congruence, we get:
   \[
   250m(k - 6) \equiv 0 \pmod{1000}
   \]
   Dividing both sides by 250, we obtain:
   \[
   m(k - 6) \equiv 0 \pmod{4}
   \]

6. For this congruence to hold for any integer \( m \), \( k - 6 \) must be a multiple of 4. Therefore, we can write:
   \[
   k - 6 = 4n \implies k = 4n + 6
   \]
   for some integer \( n \).

7. Given the constraint \( 7 \le k \le 2013 \), we substitute \( k = 4n + 6 \) and solve for \( n \):
   \[
   7 \le 4n + 6 \le 2013
   \]
   Subtracting 6 from all parts of the inequality:
   \[
   1 \le 4n \le 2007
   \]
   Dividing by 4:
   \[
   \frac{1}{4} \le n \le 501.75
   \]
   Since \( n \) must be an integer, we have:
   \[
   1 \le n \le 501
   \]

8. Therefore, \( n \) can take any integer value from 1 to 501, inclusive. This gives us 501 possible values for \( n \), and hence 501 possible values for \( k \).

The final answer is \(\boxed{501}\)

Let $m$ and $n$ be fixed positive integers. Tsvety and Freyja play a game on an infinite grid of unit square cells. Tsvety has secretly written a real number inside of each cell so that the sum of the numbers within every rectangle of size either $m$ by $n$ or $n$ by $m$ is zero. Freyja wants to learn all of these numbers.

One by one, Freyja asks Tsvety about some cell in the grid, and Tsvety truthfully reveals what number is written in it. Freyja wins if, at any point, Freyja can simultaneously deduce the number written in every cell of the entire infinite grid (If this never occurs, Freyja has lost the game and Tsvety wins).

In terms of $m$ and $n$, find the smallest number of questions that Freyja must ask to win, or show that no finite number of questions suffice.

[i]Nikolai Beluhov[/i]

1. **Understanding the Problem:**
   - Tsvety writes a real number in each cell of an infinite grid.
   - The sum of the numbers within every rectangle of size either \( m \times n \) or \( n \times m \) is zero.
   - Freyja asks for the number in specific cells and wins if she can deduce all numbers in the grid.

2. **Key Insight:**
   - If \(\gcd(m, n) = 1\), the smallest number of questions Freyja must ask is \((m-1)^2 + (n-1)^2\).
   - If \(\gcd(m, n) > 1\), no finite number of questions suffice.

3. **Proof for \(\gcd(m, n) = 1\):**
   - Consider the grid as a collection of \( m \times n \) and \( n \times m \) rectangles.
   - The sum of the numbers in each of these rectangles is zero.
   - We need to determine the minimum number of cells to ask about to deduce the entire grid.

4. **Linear Independence and Constraints:**
   - Each \( m \times n \) or \( n \times m \) rectangle provides a linear constraint.
   - For \(\gcd(m, n) = 1\), the grid can be uniquely determined by asking about \((m-1)^2 + (n-1)^2\) cells.
   - This is because the constraints provided by the rectangles are linearly independent and sufficient to solve for all cells.

5. **Detailed Calculation:**
   - Consider a subgrid of size \((m-1) \times (n-1)\).
   - Each cell in this subgrid can be determined by the constraints from the \( m \times n \) and \( n \times m \) rectangles.
   - The number of cells in this subgrid is \((m-1) \times (n-1)\).
   - Since each constraint fixes a linear combination of the variables, we need \((m-1)^2 + (n-1)^2\) constraints to determine all cells.

6. **Case for \(\gcd(m, n) > 1\):**
   - If \(\gcd(m, n) = k > 1\), the grid can have periodic patterns that cannot be determined by a finite number of questions.
   - For example, all chosen squares can be zero, and there could be a column far away with a periodic pattern that sums to zero in each \( m \times n \) or \( n \times m \) rectangle.
   - Hence, no finite number of questions can determine the entire grid.

Conclusion:
\[
\boxed{(m-1)^2 + (n-1)^2 \text{ if } \gcd(m, n) = 1, \text{ otherwise } \infty}
\]

$$
\left[\left(\frac{a-b}{x+x}\right)^{-3}\right]^{2}\left[\left(\frac{x-y}{b-a}\right)^{-2}\right]^{3}\left[\left(\frac{x-y}{x+y}\right)^{-2}\right]^{-3}=?
$$

The given expression can also be written as:

$$
\begin{gathered}
{\left[\left(\frac{x+y}{a-b}\right)^{3}\right]^{2}\left[\left(\frac{b-a}{x-y}\right)^{2}\right]^{3}\left[\left(\frac{x-y}{x+y}\right)^{2}\right]^{3}=} \\
\left(\frac{x+y}{a-b}\right)^{6}\left(\frac{b-a}{x-y}\right)^{6}\left(\frac{x-y}{x+y}\right)^{6}=\left(\frac{b-a}{a-b}\right)^{6}=(-1)^{6}=1
\end{gathered}
$$

(Woltitz Renée, Budapest, VI. district.)

The problem was also solved by: Dévai E., Eckstein V., Ehrenstein P., Freund E. Füstös P., Harsányi Z., Heimlich P., Jánosy Gy., Kiss J., Makó E., Paunz A., Pichler S., Sárközy E., Schlesinger Gy., Schwarz Gy., Schuster Gy., Szabó J., Szécsi I., Tóth B.

The answer is $\boxed{1}$.

4. On the table lie three balls, touching each other pairwise. The radii of the balls form a geometric progression with a common ratio $q \neq 1$. The radius of the middle one is 2012. Find the ratio of the sum of the squares of the sides of the triangle formed by the points of contact of the balls with the table to the sum of the sides of the triangle formed by the centers of the balls.

Answer: 4024

Let the radius of the smaller of the balls be $r$, then the radii of the others are $r q=2012$ and $r^{2}$, and the points of contact of the balls with the table are denoted as $A, B, C$ respectively. Consider the section of two balls by a plane perpendicular to the table and passing through the centers of these balls (see the figure, for the two smaller balls in size). Then for the balls shown in the figure, we get that $O A=r, O_{1} B=r q$ and $0 O_{1}=r+r q$. Further, $O_{1} D=0{ }_{1} D-D C=0{ }_{1} B-O A=r(q-1)$. From $\triangle O O_{1} D$ by the Pythagorean theorem, we get that $O D=2 r \sqrt{q}=A B$. Similarly, considering the remaining pairs of balls, we get that $A C=2 r q, B C=2 r q \sqrt{q}$, then the desired value is $\frac{4 r^{2} q+4 r^{2} q^{2}+4 r^{2} q^{3}}{r(1+q)+r\left(q+q^{2}\right)+r\left(1+q^{2}\right)}=2 r q=2 \cdot 2012=4024$

Recommendations for checking: 2 points for calculating all sides of the triangle formed by the points of contact of the balls.

The answer is $\boxed{4024}$.

## Problem Statement

Calculate the indefinite integral:

$$
\int(4 x-2) \cos 2 x \, d x
$$

## Solution

$$
\int(4 x-2) \cos 2 x d x=
$$

Let's denote:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& u=4 x-2 ; d u=4 d x \\
& d v=\cos 2 x d x ; v=\frac{1}{2} \sin 2 x
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the integration by parts formula $\int u d v=u v-\int v d u$, we get:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =(4 x-2) \cdot \frac{1}{2} \sin 2 x-\int \frac{1}{2} \sin 2 x \cdot 4 d x=(2 x-1) \sin 2 x-\int 2 \sin 2 x d x= \\
& =(2 x-1) \sin 2 x+2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \cos 2 x+C=(2 x-1) \sin 2 x+\cos 2 x+C
\end{aligned}
$$

Solution: First, we calculate the sides of triangle $ABC$: side $AC$ lies opposite the angle $30^{\circ}$, so it is half the hypotenuse, which is $1+\sqrt{3}$. Now, using the Pythagorean theorem, we calculate $BC=\sqrt{(2+2\sqrt{3})^{2}-(1+1\sqrt{3})^{2}}=\sqrt{4+8\sqrt{3}+12-1}$ $\sqrt{9+6\sqrt{3}+3}=6+2\sqrt{3}$.

Triangle $CBE$ is a right isosceles triangle with a right angle at $B$, so line $CE$ intersects $p$ at an angle of $45^{\circ}$. Triangle $ABD$ is isosceles with the angle at vertex $B$ being $120^{\circ}$ or $60^{\circ}$ depending on the position of point $D$. Therefore, line $AD$ intersects $p$ at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ or $30^{\circ}$.

Depending on the positions of points $D$ and $E$ on line $p$, triangle $DEF$ has angles of 30, 45, and $105^{\circ}$; 30, 135, and $15^{\circ}$; 60, 45, and $75^{\circ}$; or 120, 45, and $15^{\circ}$.

Consider the height $FH$ in triangle $DEF$. From the size of angle $E$, it follows that $EH=FH$.

In a right triangle with angles $60^{\circ}$ and $30^{\circ}$, the legs differ by a factor of $\sqrt{3}$, and triangle $DFH$ is precisely such a triangle. Therefore, in the first two cases, $DH=\sqrt{3}FH$, and in the remaining two cases, $DH=\frac{FH}{\sqrt{3}}$. Thus, $DE=|DH \pm EH|$ (the minus sign appears in the obtuse triangle $DEF$ with an obtuse angle at vertex $D$ or $E$), that is, when points $D$ and $E$ lie on the same side of $B$), and therefore, $DE=FH|\sqrt{3} \pm 1|$ or $DE=FH\left|1 \pm \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right|$. Express $FH$ in terms of $DE$. Side $EF=\sqrt{2}$ as the hypotenuse in the right isosceles triangle $EFH$, and in the right triangle $DFH$, the hypotenuse is twice the smaller leg.

Now, let's calculate the desired perimeter in each case separately.

Angles 30, 45, and 105 degrees. Points $D$ and $E$ lie on opposite sides of point $B$ and $DE=BD+BE=AB+BC=3\sqrt{3}+5$. $FH=\frac{DE}{\sqrt{3}+1}=\frac{3\sqrt{3}+5}{\sqrt{3}+1}=\frac{1}{2}(3\sqrt{3}+5)(\sqrt{3}-1)=2+\sqrt{3}$, then $EF=2\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{6}$ and $DF=2FH=4+2\sqrt{3}$. We get a perimeter of $2\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{6}+9+5\sqrt{3}$.

Angles 30, 135, and 15 degrees. Points $D$ and $E$ lie on the same side of point $B$ and $DE=BD-BE=AB-BC=\sqrt{3}-1$. $FH=\frac{DE}{\sqrt{3}-1}=1$, then $EF=\sqrt{2}$ and $DF=2FH=2$. We get a perimeter of $\sqrt{3}+1+\sqrt{2}$.

Angles 60, 45, and 75 degrees. Points $D$ and $E$ lie on opposite sides of point $B$ and $DE=BD+BE=AB+BC=3\sqrt{3}+5$. $FH=\frac{DE}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}+1}=\sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{DE}{\sqrt{3}+1}=2\sqrt{3}+3$, then $EF=2\sqrt{6}+3\sqrt{2}$ and $DF=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}FH=4+2\sqrt{3}$. We get a perimeter of $9+5\sqrt{3}+2\sqrt{6}+3\sqrt{2}$.

Angles 120, 45, and 15 degrees. Points $D$ and $E$ lie on the same side of point $B$ and $DE=BD-BE=AB-BC=\sqrt{3}-1$. $FH=\frac{DE}{1-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}}=\sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{DE}{\sqrt{3}-1}=\sqrt{3}$, then $EF=\sqrt{6}$, and $DF=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}FH=2$. We get a perimeter of $1+\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{6}$.

The answer is $\boxed{2\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{6}+9+5\sqrt{3},\sqrt{3}+1+\sqrt{2},9+5\sqrt{3}+2\sqrt{6}+3\sqrt{2},1+\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{6}}$.

Since $\alpha^{2}+3 \alpha-2=0$, $\alpha$ is a root of the equation $x^{2}+3 x-2=0$. Therefore,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
a_{0}+\left(a_{1}+4\right) \alpha+\left(a_{2}-10\right) \alpha^{2}+\left(a_{3}+6\right) \alpha^{3}+ \\
\left(a_{4}-1\right) \alpha^{4}+\left(a_{5}-1\right) \alpha^{5}+a_{6} \alpha^{6}+\cdots+a_{2 \cos } \alpha^{2005} \\
=M\left(\alpha^{2}+3 \alpha-2\right)=0,
\end{array}
$$

where $M$ is the quotient. Therefore,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
a_{0}+a_{1} \alpha+a_{2} \alpha^{2}+\cdots+a_{2} \cos \alpha^{2008} \\
=\alpha^{5}+\alpha^{4}-6 \alpha^{3}+10 \alpha^{2}-4 \alpha \\
=\left(\alpha^{3}-2 \alpha^{2}+2 \alpha\right)\left(\alpha^{2}+3 \alpha-2\right)=0 .

$$

The answer is $\boxed{0}$.

Let the isosceles right triangle $ABC$ with  $\angle A= 90^o$ . The points  $E$ and $F$ are taken on the ray $AC$ so that $\angle ABE   = 15^o$ and $CE = CF$. Determine the measure of the angle $CBF$.

1. Given an isosceles right triangle \( \triangle ABC \) with \( \angle A = 90^\circ \). Points \( E \) and \( F \) are on the ray \( AC \) such that \( \angle ABE = 15^\circ \) and \( CE = CF \).

2. Let \( CE = CF = a \).

3. Extend the diagram by adding point \( D \) such that \( DE = CE = a \) and \( \angle DEC = 60^\circ \).

4. By simple angle chasing, we find \( \angle BED = 45^\circ \).

5. Using the Law of Sines in \( \triangle BED \):
   \[
   \frac{BE}{\sin 30^\circ} = \frac{a}{\sin 45^\circ}
   \]
   Solving for \( BE \):
   \[
   BE = a \cdot \frac{\sin 45^\circ}{\sin 30^\circ} = a \cdot \frac{\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}}{\frac{1}{2}} = a \sqrt{2}
   \]

6. Using the Law of Cosines in \( \triangle BDE \):
   \[
   BD^2 = DE^2 + BE^2 - 2 \cdot DE \cdot BE \cdot \cos 45^\circ
   \]
   Substituting \( DE = a \) and \( BE = a \sqrt{2} \):
   \[
   BD^2 = a^2 + (a \sqrt{2})^2 - 2 \cdot a \cdot a \sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}
   \]
   Simplifying:
   \[
   BD^2 = a^2 + 2a^2 - 2a^2 = a^2
   \]
   Thus:
   \[
   BD = a
   \]

7. Since \( BD = DE \), \( \triangle BDE \) is isosceles with \( \angle BED = 45^\circ \). Therefore, \( \angle BDE = \angle BED = 45^\circ \).

8. Since \( \angle DEC = 60^\circ \), we have:
   \[
   \angle BDC = 180^\circ - \angle BDE - \angle DEC = 180^\circ - 45^\circ - 60^\circ = 75^\circ
   \]

9. Since \( \angle BCD = 15^\circ \) and \( \angle CDF = 30^\circ \), points \( B \), \( D \), and \( F \) are collinear.

10. Therefore, \( \angle CBF = \angle CBD = 15^\circ \).

The final answer is \(\boxed{15^\circ}\).

7. Let $P$ be an endpoint of a chord passing through one focus of an ellipse and perpendicular to the focal axis. If the normal line at point $P$ passes through one endpoint of the minor axis, find the eccentricity of the ellipse.

7. Let the equation of the ellipse be $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1(a>b>0)$, and since $P$ is the endpoint of the latus rectum, by Corollary 2 of Property 13, the tangent line through $P$ must pass through the intersection point $K\left(\frac{a^{2}}{c}, 0\right)$ of the directrix and the focal axis. The coordinates of point $P$ are $\left(c, \frac{b^{2}}{a}\right)$, and the coordinates of $B^{\prime}$ are $(0,-b)$. Since $P B^{\prime} \perp P K$, we have $\frac{\frac{b^{2}}{a}-(-b)}{c}=-\frac{c-\frac{a^{2}}{c}}{\frac{b^{2}}{a}}$, which simplifies to $\frac{b^{4}}{a^{2}}+\frac{b^{3}}{a}=a^{2}-c^{2}=b^{2}$, or $\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{b}{a}-1=0$. Since $\frac{b}{a}>0$, we have $\frac{b}{a}=\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{5}-1)$. Therefore, $e=\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{5}-1)}$ is the required result.

The answer is $\boxed{\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{5}-1)}}$.

Example 8. Calculate the integral

$$
I=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{2} d x}{\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{2}} \quad(a>0)
$$

Solution. Since the integrand $f(x)=\frac{x^{2}}{\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{2}}$ is an even function,

$$
I=\frac{1}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{x^{2} d x}{\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{2}}
$$

Introduce the function $f(z)=\frac{z^{2}}{\left(z^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{2}}$; which on the real axis, i.e., when $z=x$, coincides with $f(x)$. The function $f(z)$ has a pole of second order in the upper half-plane at the point $z=a i$. The residue of $f(z)$ at this pole is

$$
\operatorname{res} f(a i)=\lim _{z \rightarrow a i} \frac{d}{d z}\left[f(z)(z-a i)^{2}\right]=\lim _{z \rightarrow a i} \frac{d}{d z}\left[\frac{z^{2}}{(z+a i)^{2}}\right]=\lim _{z \rightarrow a i} \frac{2 a i z}{(z+a i)^{3}}=\frac{1}{4 a i}
$$

Using formula (4), we get

$$
I=\frac{1}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{x^{2} d x}{\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 \pi i \cdot \frac{1}{4 a i}=\frac{\pi}{4 a}
$$

Let $N=6+66+666+....+666..66$, where there are hundred $6's$ in the last term in the sum. How many times does the digit $7$ occur in the number $N$

To solve the problem, we need to determine how many times the digit $7$ appears in the number $N$, where $N$ is the sum of the sequence $6 + 66 + 666 + \ldots + 666\ldots66$ with the last term containing 100 sixes.

1. **Understanding the Pattern:**
   Let's start by examining smaller cases to identify a pattern. We denote $n$ as the number of $6$'s in the last term.

   - For $n=1$: $N = 6$
   - For $n=2$: $N = 6 + 66 = 72$
   - For $n=3$: $N = 6 + 66 + 666 = 738$
   - For $n=4$: $N = 6 + 66 + 666 + 6666 = 7404$
   - For $n=5$: $N = 6 + 66 + 666 + 6666 + 66666 = 74070$
   - For $n=6$: $N = 6 + 66 + 666 + 6666 + 66666 + 666666 = 740736$
   - For $n=7$: $N = 6 + 66 + 666 + 6666 + 66666 + 666666 + 6666666 = 7407402$
   - For $n=8$: $N = 6 + 66 + 666 + 6666 + 66666 + 666666 + 6666666 + 66666666 = 74074068$
   - For $n=9$: $N = 6 + 66 + 666 + 6666 + 66666 + 666666 + 6666666 + 66666666 + 666666666 = 740740734$
   - For $n=10$: $N = 6 + 66 + 666 + 6666 + 66666 + 666666 + 6666666 + 66666666 + 666666666 + 6666666666 = 7407407400$

2. **Identifying the Pattern:**
   From the above calculations, we observe that the number of $7$'s in $N$ increases as $n$ increases. Specifically, the number of $7$'s seems to follow the pattern where the number of $7$'s is determined by the range $2^k \leq n < 2^{k+1}$.

3. **Generalizing the Pattern:**
   We need to determine the number of $7$'s for $n = 100$. We find the largest $k$ such that $2^k \leq 100$:
   \[
   2^6 = 64 \quad \text{and} \quad 2^7 = 128
   \]
   Therefore, $64 \leq 100 < 128$, which implies $k = 6$.

4. **Conclusion:**
   According to the pattern, the number of $7$'s in $N$ when $n = 100$ is $k = 6$.

The final answer is $\boxed{6}$.

Which of the following polynomials with integer coefficients has $\sin\left(10^{\circ}\right)$ as a root?

$\text{(A) }4x^3-4x+1\qquad\text{(B) }6x^3-8x^2+1\qquad\text{(C) }4x^3+4x-1\qquad\text{(D) }8x^3+6x-1\qquad\text{(E) }8x^3-6x+1$

To determine which polynomial has $\sin(10^\circ)$ as a root, we can use the triple angle identity for sine, which states:
\[
\sin(3\theta) = 3\sin(\theta) - 4\sin^3(\theta)
\]
Let $\theta = 10^\circ$. Then, $\sin(30^\circ) = \frac{1}{2}$, and we can set up the equation:
\[
\sin(30^\circ) = 3\sin(10^\circ) - 4\sin^3(10^\circ)
\]
Let $a = \sin(10^\circ)$. Then, the equation becomes:
\[
\frac{1}{2} = 3a - 4a^3
\]
To clear the fraction, multiply both sides by 2:
\[
1 = 6a - 8a^3
\]
Rearrange the equation to form a polynomial:
\[
8a^3 - 6a + 1 = 0
\]
Thus, the polynomial with integer coefficients that has $\sin(10^\circ)$ as a root is:
\[
8x^3 - 6x + 1
\]

The final answer is $\boxed{8x^3 - 6x + 1}$

## Task Condition

Calculate the area of the figure bounded by the graphs of the functions:

$$
y=4-x^{2}, y=x^{2}-2 x
$$

## Solution

Find the abscissas of the points of intersection of the graphs of the functions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 4-x^{2}=x^{2}-2 x \\
& 2 x^{2}-2 x-4=0 \\
& x^{2}-x-2=0
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2024_05_22_aa6951b1e92815ea34b6g-04.jpg?height=1010&width=1034&top_left_y=937&top_left_x=911)

$$
\begin{aligned}
& D=(-1)^{2}-4 \cdot 1 \cdot(-2)=9 \\
& x_{1}=\frac{1-\sqrt{9}}{2 \cdot 1}=-1 \\
& x_{2}=\frac{1+\sqrt{9}}{2 \cdot 1}=2
\end{aligned}
$$

Calculate the area:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& S=\int_{-1}^{2}\left(\left(4-x^{2}\right)-\left(x^{2}-2 x\right)\right) d x=\int_{-1}^{2}\left(-2 x^{2}+2 x+4\right) d x= \\
& =\left.\left(-\frac{2 x^{3}}{3}+x^{2}+4 x\right)\right|_{-1} ^{2}=\left(-\frac{2 \cdot 2^{3}}{3}+2^{2}+4 \cdot 2\right)-
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& -\left(-\frac{2 \cdot(-1)^{3}}{3}+(-1)^{2}+4 \cdot(-1)\right)=-\frac{16}{3}+4+8-\frac{2}{3}-1+4= \\
& =-\frac{18}{3}+15=-6+15=9
\end{aligned}
$$

## Problem Kuznetsov Integrals 14-4

## Material from PlusPi

The answer is $\boxed{9}$.

In triangle $\vartriangle ABC$ with orthocenter $H$, the internal angle bisector of $\angle BAC$ intersects $\overline{BC}$ at $Y$ . Given that $AH = 4$, $AY = 6$, and the distance from $Y$ to $\overline{AC}$ is $\sqrt{15}$, compute $BC$.

1. **Identify the given information and the goal:**
   - Given: \( AH = 4 \), \( AY = 6 \), and the distance from \( Y \) to \( \overline{AC} \) is \( \sqrt{15} \).
   - Goal: Compute \( BC \).

2. **Use the properties of the orthocenter and circumcenter:**
   - The orthocenter \( H \) of \( \triangle ABC \) is the point where the altitudes intersect.
   - The circumcenter \( O \) is the center of the circumcircle of \( \triangle ABC \).
   - By a well-known lemma, \( AH = 2OM \), where \( OM \) is the perpendicular distance from \( O \) to \( BC \).

3. **Calculate \( OM \):**
   \[
   AH = 2OM \implies 4 = 2OM \implies OM = 2
   \]

4. **Relate the angles \( \angle BAC \) and \( \angle BOM \):**
   - Since \( \angle BAC = \angle BOM \), we need to find \( \tan \angle BOM \).

5. **Use the given distance from \( Y \) to \( \overline{AC} \):**
   - The distance from \( Y \) to \( \overline{AC} \) is \( \sqrt{15} \).
   - This distance is the height from \( Y \) to \( \overline{AC} \) in \( \triangle AYC \).

6. **Calculate \( \sin \angle YAC \):**
   \[
   \sin \angle YAC = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{\sqrt{15}}{6}
   \]

7. **Calculate \( \cos 2\angle YAC \):**
   \[
   \cos 2\angle YAC = 1 - 2\sin^2 \angle YAC = 1 - 2\left(\frac{\sqrt{15}}{6}\right)^2 = 1 - 2\left(\frac{15}{36}\right) = 1 - \frac{5}{6} = \frac{1}{6}
   \]

8. **Calculate \( \tan \angle BOC \):**
   - Since \( \angle BOC = 2\angle BAC \), we use the double angle formula for tangent:
   \[
   \tan \angle BOC = \sqrt{35}
   \]

9. **Calculate \( BC \):**
   - Since \( BC = 2BM \) and \( BM = 2 \times \tan \angle BOC \):
   \[
   BC = 2BM = 4\sqrt{35}
   \]

The final answer is \( \boxed{4\sqrt{35}} \).

Solution.

Domain of definition: $0<x \neq 1$.

We have: $2^{\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}-1}} \cdot 2^{-\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}+1}}=2^{\frac{2 \sqrt{x}}{x+\sqrt{x}}}, 2^{\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}-1}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}+1}}=2^{\frac{2 \sqrt{x}}{x+\sqrt{x}}}$.

Then $\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}-1}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}+1}=\frac{2 \sqrt{x}}{x+\sqrt{x}}, x-\sqrt{x}-2=0$. Solving this equation as a quadratic equation in terms of $\sqrt{x}$, we find $\sqrt{x}=-1, \varnothing$; or $\sqrt{x}=2$, from which we have $x=4$.

Answer: 4.

Example 2-34 Suppose there are two red, two blue, and two white balls. Try to list all possible combinations, assuming balls of the same color are indistinguishable.

Let $r$ represent red, $b$ represent blue, and $w$ represent white.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \left(1+r+r^{2}\right)\left(1+b+b^{2}\right)\left(1+w+w^{2}\right) \\
= & {\left[1+(r+b)+\left(r^{2}+b^{2}+r b\right)+\left(r^{2} b+r b^{2}\right)+r^{2} b^{2}\right] \cdot\left(1+w+w^{2}\right) } \\
= & 1+(r+b+w)+\left(r^{2}+b^{2}+r b+r w+b w+w^{2}\right) \\
& +\left(r^{2} b+r b^{3}+r^{2} w+w^{2} b+r b w+r w^{2}+w b^{2}\right) \\
& +\left(r^{2} w^{2}+r^{2} w b+r w^{2} b+r^{2} b^{2}+w^{2} b^{2}+r w b\right) \\
& +\left(r^{2} w^{2} b+r^{2} w b^{2}+r w^{2} b^{2}\right)+r^{2} w^{2} b^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The terms on the right side of the equation are the results of multiplying one term from each bracket on the left side, where 1 represents not taking any. $r$ or $r^{2}$ represents taking one or two red balls, respectively. Therefore, the enumeration of various combinations becomes a formal algebraic operation. For example, taking 5 balls, we have:
$$
r^{2} w^{2} b, \quad r^{2} w b, \quad r w^{2} b^{2}
$$
$r^{2} w^{2} b$ represents 2 red balls, 2 white balls, and 1 blue ball.
If we only ask how many ways there are to form a group of 4 balls, we can perform the following formal algebraic operation. Find
$$
\left(1+x+x^{2}\right)\left(1+x+x^{2}\right)\left(1+x+x^{2}\right)=\left(1+x+x^{2}\right)^{3}
$$

The coefficient of the $x^{4}$ term in the expanded form is 7, which can be estimated directly without fully expanding.
If we want to enumerate the schemes for 4 balls, we have:
$$
r^{2} w^{2}, \quad r^{2} w b, \quad r w^{2} b, \quad r^{2} b^{2}, \quad w^{2} b^{2}, \quad r^{2} b, \quad r b^{3} .
$$

The answer is $\boxed{7}$.

Let $A$ be an integer such that $100<A<1000$. Subtract the sum of the digits of $A$ from $A$, and repeat this process with the resulting number, and so on. Prove that among the numbers obtained in this way, 63 will appear.

Solution. It is known that a number written in a decimal system gives the same remainder when divided by 9 as the sum of its digits. Thus, after the first step - when we subtract the sum of the digits from $A$ - we get a number divisible by 9. Since from this point on, in each step we decrease a number divisible by 9 by a positive multiple of 9, all subsequent numbers will be divisible by 9.

Let's consider the multiples of 9 that are less than 100. In the largest of these, 99, the sum of the digits is 18, and in the others, it is 9. Now let's look at the first element of the set of numbers described in the problem that is less than 100. There is such a number, since in each step the number decreases by at least 9. This is clearly greater than 63, since the smallest multiple of 9 that, when added to 63, gives at least 100, is 36, and the sum of the digits of a three-digit number is at most 27.

If this smallest number less than 100 is 99, then 72 and then 63 follow. If, however, it is less than 99 but, as we have seen, greater than 63, then we "proceed downward in nines," and thus we will certainly reach 63.

Remark. We can reach the goal faster if we realize that the sum of the digits of a multiple of 9 that is less than 999 is at most 18. Thus, of any two consecutive multiples of 9 that are less than 100, at least one must appear in the sequence. This is also the case with the numbers 72 and 63. If 63 appears, we are done; if 72 appears, then in the next step we will get $63$.

The answer is $\boxed{63}$.

# 4. CONDITION

The sequence of numbers $\mathrm{a}_{1}, \mathrm{a}_{2}, \mathrm{a}_{3}, \ldots, \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}, \ldots$ satisfies the relations $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}-1} \cdot \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}-3}$ for $\mathrm{n}=4,5,6, \ldots$ Find $\mathrm{a}_{2019}$, given that $\mathrm{a}_{1}=1, \mathrm{a}_{2}=1, \mathrm{a}_{3}=-1$.

Solution. It is clear that all members of this sequence are equal to $\pm 1$. We find:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& a_{n}=\left(a_{n-1}\right) \cdot a_{n-3}=\left(a_{n-2} \cdot a_{n-4}\right) \cdot a_{n-3}=\left(a_{n-2}\right) \cdot a_{n-4} \cdot a_{n-3}= \\
& =\left(a_{n-3} \cdot a_{n-4}\right) \cdot a_{n-4} \cdot a_{n-3}=a_{n-3}^{2} \cdot a_{n-4} \cdot a_{n-5}=a_{n-4} \cdot a_{n-5}= \\
& =\left(a_{n-4}\right) \cdot a_{n-5}=\left(a_{n-5} \cdot a_{n-7}\right) \cdot a_{n-5}=a_{n-5}^{2} \cdot a_{n-7}=a_{n-7}
\end{aligned}
$$

That is, the sequence is periodic with a period of 7. Therefore,

$$
\mathrm{a}_{2019}=\mathrm{a}_{288 \cdot 7+3}=\mathrm{a}_{3}=-1
$$

The answer is $\boxed{-1}$.
4. The smallest positive integer $a$ that makes the inequality $\frac{1}{n+1}+\frac{1}{n+2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{2 n+1}<a-2007 \frac{1}{3}$ hold for all positive integers $n$ is $\qquad$.

Let $a_{n}=\frac{1}{n+1}+\frac{1}{n+2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{2 n+1}, a_{n+1}=\frac{1}{n+2}+\frac{1}{n+3}+\cdots+\frac{1}{2 n+3}$, then $a_{n+1}-a_{n}=\frac{1}{2 n+2}+\frac{1}{2 n+3}-\frac{1}{n+1}a_{1}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}=\frac{5}{6} \Rightarrow a>2007 \frac{1}{3}+\frac{5}{6}=2008 \frac{1}{6} \Rightarrow a \geqslant 2009$. Therefore, the smallest positive integer $a$ is 2009.

The answer is $\boxed{2009}$.

2. As shown in Figure 1, let $\triangle ABC$ be inscribed in $\odot O$, $P$ be the midpoint of arc $\overparen{BAC}$, $Q$ be the antipode of $P$, $I$ be the incenter of $\triangle ABC$, and $PI$ intersect side $BC$ at point $D$. The circumcircle of $\triangle AID$ intersects the extension of $PA$ at point $F$. Point $E$ lies on segment $PD$ such that $DE = DQ$. Let the circumradius and inradius of $\triangle ABC$ be $R$ and $r$, respectively. If $\angle AEF = \angle APE$, prove:
$$
\sin ^{2} \angle BAC = \frac{2r}{R} .
$$

2. From $\angle A E F=\angle A P E$, we know
$$
A F \cdot P F=E F^{2} \text {. }
$$

Also, from the fact that points $A, I, D, F$ are concyclic, we have
$$
\begin{array}{l}
P A \cdot P F=P I \cdot P D . \\
\text { Therefore, } P F^{2}=A F \cdot P F+P A \cdot P F \\
=E F^{2}+P I \cdot P D .
\end{array}
$$

As shown in Figure 2, connect $P Q, A Q, D F$.
Since $P Q$ is the diameter of $\odot O$ and point $I$ lies on $A Q$, we have $A I \perp A P$.
Thus, $\angle I D F=\angle I A P=90^{\circ}$, which means
$$
P F^{2}-E F^{2}=P D^{2}-E D^{2} \text {. }
$$

Combining with equation (1), we get
$$
P I \cdot P D=P D^{2}-E D^{2} \text {. }
$$

Then, $Q D^{2}=E D^{2}=P D^{2}-P I \cdot P D=I D \cdot P D$.
Therefore, $\triangle Q I D \backsim \triangle P Q D$.
Let $P Q$ bisect $B C$ perpendicularly at point $M$, and connect $M I, B P, B Q$. Then
$$
B P \perp B Q \text {. }
$$

Furthermore, since $I$ is the incenter of $\triangle A B C$, we have
$$
Q I^{2}=Q B^{2}=Q M \cdot Q P \text {. }
$$

Thus, $\triangle Q M I \backsim \triangle Q I P$.
From equations (2) and (3), we know
$$
\angle I Q D=\angle Q P D=\angle Q P I=\angle Q I M \text {. }
$$

Therefore, $M I / / Q D$.
Draw $I K \perp B C$ at point $K$. Then $I K / / P M$.
$$
\text { Hence, } \frac{P M}{I K}=\frac{P D}{I D}=\frac{P Q}{M Q} \text {. }
$$

Combining the Power of a Point theorem and the Law of Sines, we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
P Q \cdot I K=P M \cdot M Q=B M \cdot M C \\
=\left(\frac{1}{2} B C\right)^{2}=(R \sin \angle B A C)^{2} .
\end{array}
$$

Thus, $\sin ^{2} \angle B A C=\frac{P Q \cdot I K}{R^{2}}=\frac{2 R r}{R^{2}}=\frac{2 r}{R}$.

The answer is $\boxed{\sin ^{2} \angle BAC = \frac{2r}{R}}$.

Let $O$, $A$, $B$, and $C$ be points in space such that $\angle AOB=60^{\circ}$, $\angle BOC=90^{\circ}$, and $\angle COA=120^{\circ}$. Let $\theta$ be the acute angle between planes $AOB$ and $AOC$. Given that $\cos^2\theta=\frac{m}{n}$ for relatively prime positive integers $m$ and $n$, compute $100m+n$.

[i]Proposed by Michael Ren[/i]

1. **Define the problem in terms of geometry and vectors:**
   - We are given four points \(O\), \(A\), \(B\), and \(C\) in space with specific angles between them: \(\angle AOB = 60^\circ\), \(\angle BOC = 90^\circ\), and \(\angle COA = 120^\circ\).
   - We need to find the acute angle \(\theta\) between the planes \(AOB\) and \(AOC\).

2. **Set up the coordinate system:**
   - Place \(O\) at the origin \((0,0,0)\).
   - Let \(A\), \(B\), and \(C\) be points on the unit sphere centered at \(O\) (i.e., \(OA = OB = OC = 1\)).

3. **Use the given angles to find the coordinates of \(A\), \(B\), and \(C\):**
   - Assume \(A = (1, 0, 0)\).
   - Since \(\angle AOB = 60^\circ\), \(B\) can be placed at \((\frac{1}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, 0)\).
   - Since \(\angle BOC = 90^\circ\), \(C\) can be placed at \((0, 1, 0)\).

4. **Find the normal vectors to the planes \(AOB\) and \(AOC\):**
   - The normal vector to plane \(AOB\) is given by the cross product of vectors \(\overrightarrow{OA}\) and \(\overrightarrow{OB}\):
     \[
     \overrightarrow{OA} = (1, 0, 0), \quad \overrightarrow{OB} = \left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, 0\right)
     \]
     \[
     \overrightarrow{n_1} = \overrightarrow{OA} \times \overrightarrow{OB} = \begin{vmatrix}
     \mathbf{i} & \mathbf{j} & \mathbf{k} \\
     1 & 0 & 0 \\
     \frac{1}{2} & \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & 0
     \end{vmatrix} = (0, 0, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2})
     \]
   - The normal vector to plane \(AOC\) is given by the cross product of vectors \(\overrightarrow{OA}\) and \(\overrightarrow{OC}\):
     \[
     \overrightarrow{OA} = (1, 0, 0), \quad \overrightarrow{OC} = (0, 1, 0)
     \]
     \[
     \overrightarrow{n_2} = \overrightarrow{OA} \times \overrightarrow{OC} = \begin{vmatrix}
     \mathbf{i} & \mathbf{j} & \mathbf{k} \\
     1 & 0 & 0 \\
     0 & 1 & 0
     \end{vmatrix} = (0, 0, 1)
     \]

5. **Find the cosine of the angle \(\theta\) between the normal vectors:**
   - The cosine of the angle between two vectors \(\overrightarrow{n_1}\) and \(\overrightarrow{n_2}\) is given by:
     \[
     \cos \theta = \frac{\overrightarrow{n_1} \cdot \overrightarrow{n_2}}{|\overrightarrow{n_1}| |\overrightarrow{n_2}|}
     \]
   - Calculate the dot product \(\overrightarrow{n_1} \cdot \overrightarrow{n_2}\):
     \[
     \overrightarrow{n_1} \cdot \overrightarrow{n_2} = (0, 0, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}) \cdot (0, 0, 1) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}
     \]
   - Calculate the magnitudes of \(\overrightarrow{n_1}\) and \(\overrightarrow{n_2}\):
     \[
     |\overrightarrow{n_1}| = \sqrt{0^2 + 0^2 + \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \quad |\overrightarrow{n_2}| = \sqrt{0^2 + 0^2 + 1^2} = 1
     \]
   - Therefore:
     \[
     \cos \theta = \frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot 1} = 1
     \]

6. **Find \(\cos^2 \theta\):**
   \[
   \cos^2 \theta = 1
   \]

7. **Express \(\cos^2 \theta\) in the form \(\frac{m}{n}\) and compute \(100m + n\):**
   - Here, \(\cos^2 \theta = 1 = \frac{1}{1}\), so \(m = 1\) and \(n = 1\).
   - Therefore, \(100m + n = 100 \cdot 1 + 1 = 101\).

The final answer is \(\boxed{101}\).
5. If $x, y, z$ are all positive real numbers, and $x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}=1$, then the minimum value of $S=\frac{(z+1)^{2}}{2 x y z}$ is $\qquad$ .

$5.5+2 \sqrt{2}$. 
Since $2 x y \leqslant x^{2}+y^{2}=1-z^{2}$, we have,
$$
\begin{aligned}
S & =\frac{(z+1)^{2}}{2 x y z} \geqslant \frac{(z+1)^{2}}{z\left(1-z^{2}\right)} \\
& =\frac{z+1}{(1-z) z}=\frac{z+1}{[2-(z+1)][(z+1)-1]} \\
& =\frac{1}{3-\left[(z+1)+\frac{2}{z+1}\right]} \\
& \geqslant \frac{1}{3-2 \sqrt{2}}=3+2 \sqrt{2} .
\end{aligned}
$$

The equality holds if and only if $z=\sqrt{2}-1, x=y=\sqrt{\sqrt{2}-1}$.

The answer is $\boxed{3+2 \sqrt{2}}$.

On the Moon, coins with denominations of 1, 15, and 50 ferthings are in circulation. Nезнайка paid for a purchase with several coins and received change - one more coin than he paid with. What is the smallest possible price of the purchase?

## Hint

The remainder when 1, 15, and 50 are divided by 7 is 1.

## Solution

The remainder when the denomination of each coin is divided by 7 is 1. Let Nезнайка pay with $k$ coins totaling $A$ and receive change $C$. Then $A \equiv k(\bmod 7)$, and

$C \equiv k+1(\bmod 7)$. Therefore, the price of the purchase $A-C \equiv 6(\bmod 7)$. Consequently, this price cannot be less than 6 ferthings.

An example where the purchase costs 6 ferthings: Nезнайка paid with two coins - 1 ferthing and 50 ferthings, and received change with three coins of 15 ferthings each.

## Answer

6 ferthings.

The answer is $\boxed{6}$.
5. Three circles with radii 1, 2, 3 touch each other externally at three points. Find the radius of the circle passing through these three points.

Answer: 1.

Solution: Let $\mathrm{O}_{1}, \mathrm{O}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{O}_{3}$ be the centers of the given circles, K, M, N the points of tangency, such that $\mathrm{O}_{1} \mathrm{~K}=\mathrm{O}_{1} \mathrm{~N}=1, \mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{~K}=\mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{M}=2$ and $\mathrm{O}_{3} \mathrm{~N}=$ $\mathrm{O}_{3} \mathrm{M}=3$. It is known that when two circles touch, the point of tangency lies on the segment connecting their centers. Therefore, K, M, N are points on the sides of the triangle $\mathrm{O}_{1} \mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$. Note that the desired circle coincides with the circle inscribed in the triangle $\mathrm{O}_{1} \mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$. Indeed, since the equalities $\mathrm{O}_{1} \mathrm{~K}=\mathrm{O}_{1} \mathrm{~N}, \mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{~K}=\mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{M}$ and $\mathrm{O}_{3} \mathrm{~N}=\mathrm{O}_{3} \mathrm{M}$ hold, the points $\mathrm{K}, \mathrm{M}, \mathrm{N}$ are the points of tangency of the inscribed circle with the sides of the triangle $\mathrm{O}_{1} \mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$.

Let $r$ be the desired radius, write the area of the triangle $\mathrm{O}_{1} \mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ in terms of the perimeter and $r$, and using Heron's formula. We get the equation:

$$
\frac{1}{2}(3+4+5) r=\sqrt{6(6-3)(6-4)(6-5)} \Rightarrow 6 r=6 \Rightarrow r=1
$$

Example 6 (CMO-5 Test) Let $x$ be a natural number. If a sequence of natural numbers $x_{0}=1, x_{1}, x_{2}, \cdots$, $x_{l}=x$, satisfies $x_{i-1}<x_{i}, x_{i-1} \mid x_{i}, i=1,2, \cdots, l$, then $\left\{x_{0}, x_{1}, x_{2}, \cdots, x_{l}\right\}$ is called a divisor chain of $x$, $l$ is the length of this divisor chain, and $L(x)$ and $R(x)$ represent the length of the longest divisor chain of $x$ and the number of the longest divisor chains of $x$, respectively.
For $x=5^{k} \cdot 31^{m} \cdot 1990^{n}(k, m, n$ are natural numbers), try to find $L(x)$ and $R(x)$.

For any natural number
$$
x=p_{1}^{\alpha_{1}} p_{2}^{\alpha_{2}} \cdots p_{n}^{\alpha_{n}},
$$

where $p_{1}, p_{2}, \cdots, p_{n}$ are distinct prime numbers, and $\alpha_{1}, \alpha_{2}, \cdots, \alpha_{n}$ are positive integers.
Clearly, the factor chain of $x$ exists and is finite, thus there must be a longest factor chain.
Let $\left\{x_{0}, x_{1}, \cdots, x_{t}\right\}$ be a longest factor chain of $x$. We prove that $\frac{x_{i}}{x_{i-1}}$ must be a prime number $(i=1,2, \cdots, t)$.
In fact, if there exists $i(1 \leqslant i \leqslant t)$ such that $\frac{x_{i}}{x_{i-1}}$ is not a prime, we can assume
$$
\frac{x_{i}}{x_{i-1}}=q_{1} q_{2},
$$

where $q_{1}, q_{2}$ are positive integers greater than 1, then
$$
\left\{x_{0}, x_{1}, \cdots, x_{i-1}, q_{1} x_{i-1}, x_{i}, x_{i+1}, \cdots, x_{i}\right\}
$$

is also a factor chain of $x$.
This contradicts the fact that $\left\{x_{0}, x_{1}, \cdots, x_{i-1}, x_{i}, \cdots, x_{t}\right\}$ is the longest factor chain.
Therefore, for any $1 \leqslant i \leqslant t, \frac{x_{i}}{x_{i-1}}$ must be a prime factor of $x$, thus
$$
t=L(x)=\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}+\cdots+\alpha_{n}.
$$

Conversely, if $\left\{x_{0}, x_{1}, \cdots, x_{m}\right\}$ is a factor chain of $x$, and for any $1 \leqslant i \leqslant m, \frac{x_{i}}{x_{i-1}}$ is a prime, by the definition of a factor chain we have
$$
m=\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}+\cdots+\alpha_{n}=L(x).
$$

Thus, $\left\{x_{0}, x_{1}, \cdots, x_{m}\right\}$ must be a longest factor chain.
Therefore, starting from 1 and multiplying a prime factor of $x$ step by step until reaching $x$, we get a longest factor chain of $x$, and different orders of multiplying different prime factors result in different longest factor chains, hence
$$
R(x)=\frac{\left(\alpha_{1}+\cdots+\alpha_{n}\right)!}{\alpha_{1}!\cdot \cdots \cdot \alpha_{n}!}
$$

For $x=5^{k} \cdot 31^{m} \cdot 1990^{n}=2^{n} \cdot 5^{k+n} \cdot 31^{m} \cdot 199^{n}$.
Then $L(x)=3 n+k+m$,
$$
R(x)=\frac{(3 n+k+m)!}{(n!)^{2} m!(k+n)!}
$$

The answer is $\boxed{L(x)=3n+k+,\quadR(x)=\frac{(3n+k+)!}{(n!)^2!(k+n)!}}$.

10. (5 points) A basket of apples is divided into two portions, A and B. The ratio of the number of apples in A to the number of apples in B is $27: 25$, with A having more apples than B. If at least 4 apples are taken from A and added to B, then B will have more apples than A. How many apples are there in the basket?

【Analysis】 "If at least 4 are taken from A and added to B, then B will have more than A" means that if 3 are taken from A and added to B, then B will not have more than A.

【Solution】 According to the problem:
If 4 are taken from A and added to B, then B will have more than A; if 3 are taken from A and added to B, then B will not have more than A. Let A have $27n$, and B have $25n$,
then: $\left\{\begin{array}{l}27n-4<25n+4 \\ 27n-3 \geqslant 25n+3\end{array}\right.$
we get $3 \leqslant n<4$,
so $n=3$,
the total number of apples is: $27n+25n=156$,
therefore, there are 156 apples in the basket.

The answer is $\boxed{156}$.

Let $P$ be a regular polygon with 2026 sides. A diagonal of $P$ is said to be good if its endpoints divide the perimeter of $P$ into two parts, each having an odd number of sides of $P$. The sides of $P$ are also considered good. Suppose that $P$ has been subdivided into triangles by 2023 diagonals that do not intersect each other inside $P$. How many, at most, such a subdivision can contain isosceles triangles with two good sides?

## Solutions to the exercises

Translate the above text into English, preserving the original text's line breaks and format, and output the translation directly.

A triangle will be said to be isobon if it is isosceles and has two good sides. By grouping the 2026 sides of $P$ into 1013 groups of two consecutive sides, 1013 isobon triangles are formed, which can then be integrated into a triangulation.

Now consider any triangulation, and we set out to prove that it does not contain more than 1013 isobon triangles. In the following, we will identify the sides of $P$ with good diagonals. We then consider the bipartite graph $\mathcal{G}=(A \sqcup B, E)$ where $A$ is the set of triangles in the triangulation, $B$ is the set of good diagonals, and $E$ is the set of pairs consisting of a triangle $a \in A$ and a good side of $a$.

Finally, we associate an integer with each good vertex $b \in B$, that is, each good diagonal, as follows: if $b$ separates $P$ into two parts with $x$ and $y$ vertices, we associate the integer $\min \{x, y\}$ with $b$. Consequently, a triangle $a \in A$ is isobon if it is adjacent to two vertices that have been associated with the same integer.

Any triangle $a \in A$ divides $P$ into four regions: $t$ and three other regions that do not share any side between them. Therefore, removing $a$ from the graph $\mathcal{G}$ disconnects $\mathcal{G}$, which means that $\mathcal{G}$ is acyclic: $c^{\prime}$ is a forest.

Moreover, every triangle $a \in A$ has 0 or 2 good sides; its degree in $\mathcal{G}$ is therefore 0 or 2. Consequently, each connected component of $\mathcal{G}$ is in fact a path, which starts from one side of $P$ and ends at another side of $P$: there are 1013 such paths. Now, when traversing a path, the integers associated with the vertices $b \in B$ encountered start by strictly increasing from 1, then they strictly decrease until they are 1 again. The only time two vertices with the same number could have been encountered was when this number was the highest possible on the path. We conclude that each path contains at most one isobon triangle, and therefore that the maximum sought is 1013.
